content.text: What Pipeline Stages Are for Agentic AI

A CI/CD pipeline for an agentic AI application passes through a defined sequence of stages that transform a code commit into a verified, deployed artifact. The stages for agentic AI follow the same skeleton as traditional software pipelines—lint, test, deploy—but insert a new and expensive middle layer: the eval harness. This stage runs the application's prompt-plus-model combination against a versioned dataset of behavioral test cases and fails the build if quality thresholds are not met. Everything downstream of the eval stage benefits from the guarantee that the shipped agent meets its quality contract.

The stages also differ in what they treat as a deployable artifact. Traditional pipelines build and ship source code compiled into a binary or container. Agentic AI pipelines ship a composite artifact: the application container, a pinned model identifier, a pinned prompt version from a prompt registry, and a pinned eval dataset version used to validate that configuration. All four components must be tracked together to make a release reproducible and rollback-safe.
Why It Matters

Without a structured stage sequence, teams ship LLM-based features with no systematic quality gate between 'code merged' and 'users affected.' A prompt change that causes a 12% regression in answer accuracy reaches production silently because there was no eval stage to catch it. Conversely, a team with stages but no ordering discipline finds that integration tests run before evals have passed—wasting compute on downstream stages for a build that would have been rejected earlier.

Stage gates make the promotion contract explicit. A build cannot advance from the eval stage to integration testing unless pass_rate meets the threshold. It cannot advance from integration to staging without a clean integration run. This sequential dependency turns implicit quality expectations into enforced checkpoints, which makes releases predictable and regressions traceable to the commit that introduced them.
Benefits
Fail fast and cheap — lint and unit tests run first (seconds, free) so obvious breaks don't consume eval API tokens; expensive LLM calls happen only after cheap checks pass.
Reproducible releases — pinning model ID, prompt version, and dataset version inside the pipeline means any historical build can be re-run and will produce the same results.
Clear regression signal — because each stage has a binary pass/fail gate, a failing build immediately identifies which stage broke, narrowing the investigation to a bounded problem space.
Artifact provenance — passing artifacts from stage to stage (results JSON, container image SHA, eval summary) creates a chain of custody that links every production deploy to its validation evidence.
Decoupled teams — prompt engineers, model engineers, and application engineers can iterate independently; the pipeline resolves their contributions at each gate.
Trade-offs
Longer pipeline wall time — adding an eval harness stage that calls an LLM for each of 1,000 test cases adds 5–20 minutes to a pipeline that previously took 3 minutes.
LLM API cost per CI run — running evals in CI costs money proportional to token count and case count; a team with 10 engineers pushing frequently can accumulate meaningful API spend.
Non-deterministic gates — pass rate can vary slightly between runs due to model temperature; teams must decide whether to use temperature=0 for stability or accept small variance.
Complexity overhead — more stages mean more pipeline YAML, more potential points of failure, and more configuration to maintain as the project evolves.
The Six Stages: Mechanics

Stage 1 (Code Commit) triggers on a push to a feature branch or main. It performs a secret scan (e.g., trufflehog) and validates that commit messages meet the project convention. No code is run. Stage 2 (Lint and Unit Tests) runs static analysis, type checking, and fast unit tests that mock all LLM calls. This stage must complete in under 3 minutes to keep developer feedback tight. Prompt template syntax is validated here using a lightweight YAML schema check against the prompt registry.

Stage 3 (Eval Harness) is the LLM-specific stage. It checks out the pinned dataset version, resolves the prompt version from the registry, confirms the model identifier is on the allowed-models list, and runs each eval case against the live model. Results are written to a JSON artifact. A gate script checks pass_rate, regression count, and coverage against the thresholds in the dataset manifest. Only a PASS result allows the pipeline to continue. Stage 4 (Integration Tests) tests the agent's tool calls, memory reads and writes, and multi-step conversation flows in an isolated environment with real (sandboxed) tool endpoints.

Stage 5 (Staging Deploy) deploys the container image—built with the pinned model config baked in—to the staging environment. Smoke tests verify the agent endpoint responds correctly to a fixed set of probing queries. A canary eval sub-run may re-score a small random sample of eval cases in staging to catch environment-specific issues. Stage 6 (Production Deploy) uses blue-green or rolling deployment with a traffic-shift gate: if error rate or latency exceeds thresholds in the first 10% of traffic, the pipeline halts and rolls back automatically.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Six pipeline stages for agentic AI: code commit, lint/unit, eval harness (LLM-specific), integration tests, staging deploy, production deploy with artifact flow and comparison to traditional pipelines

content.text: Mental Model: The Evaluation Tollbooth

Think of each stage gate as a tollbooth on a highway. A vehicle (build) passes through each booth only if it meets the booth's criteria. If the vehicle fails the second booth's inspection, it never reaches the third. Adding the eval harness stage is like adding a new specialized tollbooth for LLM behavior between the mechanical inspection (unit tests) and the road test (integration tests). The booth has a different inspector—an automated judge scoring LLM outputs—and a different pass criterion—a statistical quality threshold rather than a binary assertion.
LLM-Specific Stage Differences
Prompt registry check — the eval stage verifies the prompt version referenced in the application config exists in the prompt registry and is not deprecated; traditional stages have no equivalent.
Model version pinning — the pipeline enforces that the model ID in the application config matches an approved list; a PR that silently upgrades the model to an unvalidated version is rejected.
Probabilistic gate thresholds — instead of a boolean assertion, the eval stage uses a pass rate threshold (e.g., >= 88%), requiring the team to reason statistically about quality.
API cost per stage run — the eval stage incurs real token costs; teams add result caching and selective re-evaluation to control spend without eliminating the gate.
When to Use Sequential vs Parallel Stage Execution

Sequential execution (each stage waits for the previous to complete) is the default and the safest: cheap stages gate expensive ones, preventing wasted spend. Parallelism should be introduced only within a stage, not between stages with dependencies. For example, multiple eval category subsets can run in parallel within the eval harness stage, but the integration test stage must still wait for the full eval harness stage to pass.
How to Implement: Step by Step
Define the stage sequence in a central workflow file (GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, or CircleCI). Use needs: dependencies to enforce ordering.
Configure fail-fast: true so that a failing eval stage cancels any parallel unit test retries and prevents downstream stages from starting.
Store the pinned model ID, prompt version, and dataset version in a single eval_config.yaml committed to the application repo.
Build the container image once in Stage 2 and pass its SHA digest as an output variable to all subsequent stages; never rebuild the image in staging or production.
Upload eval results as a CI artifact so they are available for debugging, compliance audits, and as input to the promotion gate script.
Add a manual approval step between the staging and production stages for high-risk releases (major prompt rewrites, model upgrades) and skip it for patch-level changes.
Example: GitHub Actions Six-Stage Pipeline

# .github/workflows/agentic-ai-pipeline.yml
name: Agentic AI Delivery Pipeline
on:
  push:
    branches: [main, 'feat/**']

jobs:
  # Stage 1: Code hygiene
  code-commit:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    outputs:
      sha: ${{ github.sha }}
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Secret scan
        run: trufflehog filesystem . --no-update --fail

  # Stage 2: Lint + unit tests
  lint-unit:
    needs: code-commit
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    outputs:
      image_sha: ${{ steps.build.outputs.sha }}
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: pip install -e .[dev]
      - run: ruff check . && mypy src/
      - run: pytest tests/unit/ -q
      - name: Validate prompt registry schema
        run: python scripts/validate_prompts.py --registry prompts/
      - name: Build container image
        id: build
        run: |
          docker build -t app:${{ github.sha }} .
          echo "sha=${{ github.sha }}" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT

  # Stage 3: LLM eval harness (the unique stage)
  eval-harness:
    needs: lint-unit
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    env:
      OPENAI_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.OPENAI_API_KEY }}
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: pip install -e .[eval]
      - name: Fetch eval dataset
        run: |
          git clone --depth 1 --branch evals/v2.3.1 \
            https://x-token:${{ secrets.EVALS_TOKEN }}@github.com/org/evals.git evals
      - name: Run eval harness
        run: |
          python scripts/run_evals.py \
            --config eval_config.yaml \
            --dataset evals/cases/ \
            --output results/eval_results.json
      - name: Check promotion gate
        run: |
          python scripts/check_promotion_gate.py \
            --results results/eval_results.json \
            --manifest evals/manifest.yaml
      - uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: eval-results
          path: results/

  # Stage 4: Integration tests
  integration-tests:
    needs: eval-harness
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: pip install -e .[test]
      - run: pytest tests/integration/ -q --timeout=120

  # Stage 5: Staging deploy
  staging-deploy:
    needs: integration-tests
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment: staging
    steps:
      - name: Deploy to staging
        run: |
          kubectl set image deployment/agent \
            agent=app@sha256:${{ needs.lint-unit.outputs.image_sha }}
      - name: Run smoke tests
        run: pytest tests/smoke/ --base-url https://staging.example.com

  # Stage 6: Production deploy (main only)
  production-deploy:
    needs: staging-deploy
    if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment: production
    steps:
      - name: Blue-green deploy
        run: |
          kubectl set image deployment/agent-blue \
            agent=app@sha256:${{ needs.lint-unit.outputs.image_sha }}
      - name: Shift 10% traffic
        run: kubectl apply -f k8s/traffic-split-10pct.yaml
      - name: Watch error rate
        run: python scripts/watch_error_rate.py --duration 300 --max-rate 0.01
Example: GitLab CI Equivalent

# .gitlab-ci.yml
stages:
  - commit
  - lint
  - eval
  - integration
  - staging
  - production

variables:
  EVAL_DATASET_VERSION: "2.3.1"
  MODEL_ID: "gpt-4o-2024-11-20"

secret-scan:
  stage: commit
  script:
    - trufflehog filesystem . --no-update --fail

lint-and-unit:
  stage: lint
  script:
    - pip install -e .[dev]
    - ruff check . && mypy src/
    - pytest tests/unit/ -q

eval-harness:
  stage: eval
  variables:
    OPENAI_API_KEY: $OPENAI_API_KEY
  script:
    - pip install -e .[eval]
    - git clone --depth 1 --branch evals/v${EVAL_DATASET_VERSION} $EVALS_REPO evals
    - python scripts/run_evals.py --config eval_config.yaml --output results/eval_results.json
    - python scripts/check_promotion_gate.py --results results/eval_results.json
  artifacts:
    paths:
      - results/
    expire_in: 30 days

integration:
  stage: integration
  needs: [eval-harness]
  script:
    - pytest tests/integration/ -q

staging:
  stage: staging
  environment: staging
  script:
    - helm upgrade --install agent ./chart --set image.tag=$CI_COMMIT_SHA
    - pytest tests/smoke/ --base-url $STAGING_URL

production:
  stage: production
  environment: production
  only:
    - main
  when: manual
  script:
    - helm upgrade agent ./chart --set image.tag=$CI_COMMIT_SHA --set traffic.weight=10
Real-World Example: Multi-Team Agentic Platform

A platform team ships a customer support agent. Three sub-teams contribute: a prompt team that manages the system prompt in a versioned registry, a model team that periodically evaluates new model versions for upgrade, and an application team that writes tool integrations. The pipeline's eval harness stage resolves the current prompt version from the registry (v1.4.2), the current model pin from eval_config.yaml (gpt-4o-2024-11-20), and the dataset from the evals repo (v2.3.1). When the prompt team proposes a new system prompt, their PR triggers a pipeline that runs all 1,100 eval cases. The results show a 2% improvement. The PR is promoted through staging and merged.

When the model team proposes upgrading to a new model, they update the model pin in eval_config.yaml. The eval harness re-runs all cases against the new model. Pass rate drops from 91% to 84%, below the 88% threshold. The gate blocks the PR. The model team investigates the failing cases, discovers a tool-calling format change in the new model, updates the tool-use prompt, and re-runs. Pass rate recovers to 92%. The PR then passes all stages and ships.
Common Mistakes
Running eval before unit tests — eval costs API tokens; running it on code that would have failed a 30-second unit test is pure waste.
Rebuilding the container image in each stage — the image should be built once and passed as a SHA digest; rebuilding produces different layers and defeats immutable artifact guarantees.
No stage gate between staging and production — deploying to production immediately after staging with no gate (or no smoke test wait) eliminates the buffer for catching staging-specific failures.

Not pinning the model ID — a pipeline that uses 'latest' for the model endpoint will produce different eval scores as the provider silently upgrades the model, making regressions impossible to attribute.

Skipping the eval stage on 'minor' prompt changes — users of LLM systems know that even a small wording change can shift output distribution significantly; the eval stage must run on all prompt changes.
Best Practices
Cache eval results keyed on (dataset_version, model_id, prompt_hash) and skip re-scoring unchanged (case, config) pairs to reduce eval stage cost by 70–90% on typical PRs.
Pin all four components—model ID, prompt version, dataset version, application image SHA—in a single eval_config.yaml file committed to the repo.
Use environment: staging and environment: production in GitHub Actions to enforce required reviewers before sensitive deployments.
Run a nightly full eval pipeline (no caching) on main to catch model API drift that would not be visible in incremental PR runs.
Performance and Scalability

Pipeline wall time grows linearly with eval dataset size if cases run serially. For datasets above 200 cases, parallel eval execution within the eval harness stage is essential. The harness shards cases across multiple workers, each making independent API calls. Fan-out/fan-in logic collects partial results and merges them before the gate check. This technique can reduce a 20-minute serial eval to 3–4 minutes with 8 parallel workers. The Parallel Eval and Test Pipeline Design topic covers this in detail.
Tooling and Ecosystem
GitHub Actions — matrix and needs: directives make it the most common choice for defining stage sequences; environments provide manual approval gates.
GitLab CI — stages: keyword with needs: for DAG-style ordering; built-in artifact passing; cache: for eval result caching.
Braintrust CI — purpose-built eval CI that integrates natively with the eval harness stage; provides pass/fail status checks on PRs with detailed per-case diffs.
Weights & Biases Artifacts — tracks model config and dataset version as lineage artifacts, linking them to the CI run that produced the eval results.
Comparison with Alternatives
Pipeline Topology
Eval Stage
Wall Time
Best for
Traditional (no eval)
None
2–5 min
Non-LLM apps only
Eval at end (post-integration)
After integration
Wastes compute; eval feedback is late
Eval after lint (recommended)
Before integration
Most teams; fail fast before expensive integration
Eval parallel with integration
Concurrent
8–12 min
Speed-optimized teams willing to pay for wasted integration runs
Security and Reliability
Restrict which actors can trigger production deploys — use GitHub Actions environments with required reviewers; CI runners should not have direct kubectl access to production.

Use OIDC-based cloud credentials in CI rather than long-lived service account keys; scope each stage's permissions to exactly what it needs (eval stage needs only LLM API access, not cloud deploy access).
Sign container images and verify signatures at deploy time; this ensures that the image deployed to production is exactly the one built and validated in the CI pipeline.
Set API rate limit budgets for the eval stage and fail the stage if budget is exceeded, preventing a runaway eval from incurring unbounded LLM spend.
Key Takeaways
Agentic AI pipelines add one new stage—the eval harness—between unit tests and integration tests; this is the primary structural difference from traditional CI/CD.
Order stages from cheapest to most expensive: secret scan first, then lint/unit, then eval, then integration, then deploy.
Pass a single immutable container image SHA from the build stage through all subsequent stages; never rebuild.
Pin the model ID, prompt version, and dataset version in eval_config.yaml; a pipeline is reproducible only when all four components (code, model, prompt, dataset) are pinned.
Stage gates are binary: pass_rate meets threshold and zero regressions allows advancement; anything else blocks the pipeline.
Cache eval results to control LLM API cost; a nightly uncached run catches model API drift that incremental caching would miss.
